title: Conventional Qualities
wikitext:
[]: Conventional Qualities             Quote Post by Felipe » Mon Feb 04, 2013 5:15 pm I don't think it's a stretch to say that Evangelion's most appreciated quality is its postmodern side: it is fundamentally a show about overcoming the hardships of being alive and having the courage to suffer, love, be loved, and live. It is a story that I think could be told without giant robots or apocalyptic conspiracies, because Anno's real concern was about what it means to be a living human being. That being said, I feel like the more "mundane" or conventional qualities are kindda taken for granted, and don't get appreciated as they should be. Namely: - Evangelion is a fantastic episodic narrative. Each episode brings a new conflict or enemy, and we're always at the edge of our proverbial seat to find out what happens next, and how our hero will get out of the current tough situation. - It's great action. The fights against the Angels are awesome in every sense of the word. They feel real , and we feel like what's at stake really matters. It succeeds in a way that I've rarely seen even in live action films. - Great Characterization. All characters feel real enough that we care deeply about them and even recognize traits in ourselves or in people we know, but at the same time, they are unique enough to be interesting. Any thoughts?

[]:             Quote Post by The Killer of Heroes » Mon Feb 04, 2013 5:52 pm I find it kind of hard to talk about these "mundane" parts on their own, because they really reinforce the more surreal parts of the series, and vice versa. It's neat to me how in the second half of the show, these parts gradually get chipped away and distorted, becoming, well, the Eva we know and love. :P Still, I agree that its all good stuff. Its always nice to see those moments where the characters don't think their world is crumbling beneath them, and to see them actually happy, crushing Angels each week. It creates personal stakes for the characters in the show, since they actually have something they can lose; a sense of happy stability. If the whole show was just grim grim grim the whole way through, than it would be a little desensitizing when bad shit actually does go down.

[]:             Quote Post by Lance of LoL » Mon Feb 04, 2013 6:11 pm I think you're right about the action. It's rarely a case of the more played out "Who has more power" type thing you so often see in anime. The Angels often present themselves in a manner that requires thought to overcome, not just brute force, though when 01 goes Berserk it helps. XD Though it does sometimes feel a bit forced. Like talking about Thermal Expansion in the Volcano episode, it's generally well thought out and it's often through the strategic planning that you actually get a feel for Misato as being worth her title.

[]:             Quote Post by xdiesp » Mon Feb 04, 2013 6:32 pm I for one, hold especially dear the "angel of the week" episodic structure because it comes from the deconstruction of the giant robot genre. Which I intensely followed in the 70s and 80s. And EVA never had a final boss like the ones in those shows: say, Mazinger or Devilman or Daitan 3. Yeah, they were pretty much like a videogame last boss. In that respect, I could even accept Episode 24 as a valid ending. "Threat neutralized" and that's it, at this point at most in the old shows you would see the hero sailing back to his home planet.

[]:             Quote Post by Felipe » Mon Feb 04, 2013 7:27 pm Something I forgot to mention is how we only care about the deep, introspective latter part of the show is because we spent so much time getting to know and understand these characters, and really feeling like Tokyo-3 is a real place. If it were pure navel-gazing from the very beginning, it'd feel kindda like modern art, I think: purely cerebral, but with no emotion attached to it.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Tue Feb 05, 2013 2:09 am xdiesp wrote: In that respect, I could even accept Episode 24 as a valid ending. "Threat neutralized" and that's it, at this point at most in the old shows you would see the hero sailing back to his home planet.

[]:             Quote Post by Olmer » Tue Feb 05, 2013 4:09 am One thing that people rarely talk about when it comes to Evangelion, is just how well directed it is. It only stroke me after the third viewing, I think, as I expected earlier episodes to be kinda boring. Instead they turned out to be more interesting than ever. The knowledge I got from repeated viewings let me appreciate many scenes in a new way, and find new beauty in them. At this point, I don't even consider the second half of the show to be better than the first and I percieve the entire NGE to be much more "homogenous" than I once thought. Another thing related to the direction, which is rarely mentioned, is just how well NGE manages to merge slice of life elements with psychological stuff and mecha action. It's really hard to keep the proper mood up all the time when you're forced to transition between school/house/base/cockpit/mind of the pilot 15 times per episode. EVA does it seemingly effortlessly. Some people find the "monster-of-the-week" episodes irritating, or have some issues with the pacing, but honestly I wouldn't have it any other way. When it comes to EVA, people talk waaaaaay too much about religious symbolism, how controversial the show was, how there were budget problems, how influential the show turned out to be, how Anno was depressed when creating it, how it shifted the tone in the middle, how "bad" the ending was etc. I would even go as far as saying that the show is "underrated" in the sense that it rarely gets praised just for how well it was made. People know that it's popular/influential/controversial, but they rarely say it's technically brilliant. It's the other way round with Studio Ghibli films (at least that was my impression). Everybody and their dog will tell you just how well made those movies are. That's like the first thing you'll hear about them everytime you ask. I have a friend who only watched Howl's Moving Castle and only once. He apparently never became a fan and never cared enough to watch more from Ghibli. Still, if I ask him about it, he'll praise the execution to the high heavens. It's just that certain productions have those "labels" sticked to them. Ghibli has the "really well made movies for the entire family" label while EVA (at least among my friends) has the "show that nobody understands and nobody talks about" label. Nobody's making fun of it though. I got the impression that in USA there was a time when it was a cool thing to make fun of EVA because of how incomprehensible it was, right?

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Tue Feb 05, 2013 3:19 pm While people don't talk much about it, at least not in here, i don't think you'll find many who would disagree with the idea that Anno and the guys at GAINAX were exceptionally good at their jobs, and NGE is often praised for it's technical quality as well.
